architecture.summary: LaRS learns latent reasoning skills from rationales in an example bank using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). The CVAE has three components: an encoder that maps question-rationale pairs to latent skill embeddings, a decoder that reconstructs rationales from questions and sampled skills, and a reasoning policy that predicts required skills for new questions. During demonstration selection, the reasoning policy encodes a test question to a skill vector, and examples are ranked by cosine similarity of their encoded skills to the test question's skill, enabling automatic selection of in-context examples for chain-of-thought prompting without requiring external LLM skill annotations.
trained_components: reasoning policy network (question to skill distribution)

biblio.title: LaRS: Latent Reasoning Skills for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
biblio.venue: Findings of Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2024

claims.statement: LaRS outperforms Skill-KNN-zero in 18 out of 20 experiments across all four benchmarks and five backbone models tested
LaRS requires no LLM inferences (approximately $30 savings per 1k examples) and reduces computing time by 1.5 hours per 1k examples during pre-processing
claims.unit: dollars per 1k examples; 1.5 hours per 1k examples

claims.statement: LaRS achieves more than 100% less cost per input query compared to Skill-KNN-zero
On TabMWP with 30% of rationales replaced by random rationales, Skill-KNN-zero suffers a 3% performance drop while LaRS experiences only 0.1% performance drop
On COGS with 30% of rationales replaced by random rationales, Skill-KNN-zero suffers an 11.7% performance drop while LaRS experiences only 1.9% performance drop
LaRS achieves 78.1% accuracy on TabMWP with gpt-3.5-turbo, matching Skill-KNN-zero (77.7%) and approaching oracle Retrieval-R (77.4%)

Under Assumption 1 (example bank sampled from optimal conditional distribution) and Assumption 2 (humans and LLMs are expert rationale generators given reasoning skills and questions), skill-based selection yields the optimal conditional distribution of rationales given questions

LaRS does not require powerful LLMs and achieves similar performance boosts for smaller backbone models like Falcon-40B-Instruct compared to Skill-KNN-zero, whereas Skill-KNN-small (using Falcon-40B-instruct for skill generation) suffers significant performance drops across all benchmarks
